title: FOMO: the real cost of chasing a green candle
_note: EVERGREEN (warm-up bank for ad traffic). No invented numbers/prices/dates.
body:
🧠 FOMO — the real cost of chasing a green candle

The scene
A candle rips. Your chat lights up. Everyone's 'in'. Your finger hovers. The fear isn't of losing money — it's of being the one who watched it go. So you click at the top of the move, right as it's about to breathe back.

What actually happened
When you chase a vertical candle, you're not catching the move. You're buying the exit liquidity of the crowd that got in early. Someone has to sell into that spike for it to exist — and the late chaser is who they're selling to. You became the bid that lets early money walk away clean.

The part that stings
That exact extended setup — the one screaming 'easy, everyone's in' — is often the one WE faded. Crowded, stretched, funding leaning hard one way: that's not a green light to us, it's a flag. The crowded side gets liquidated first. Chasing the candle puts you on the crowded side at the worst possible price.

The fix
• FOMO is an emotion, not a signal. Name it and it loses half its grip.
• If your reason for entering is 'it's already moving', you have no reason. A move in progress is not a setup.
• A missed trade costs you nothing. A chased trade costs you the wick. There's always another candle.
• Pre-decide your entry zone. If price left it, the trade left with it. Let it go.

The market pays the patient at the expense of the impatient. FOMO is just the toll the impatient pay — to the people who waited.

#MindOverMarket
